coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of oxygen defect-rich pencil-like ZnO nanorods with CDots and Ag co-enhanced photocatalytic activity for tetracycline hydrochloride degradation
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Tianren
Liu, Yinglu
Li, Mingyu
Jiang, Jingjing
Gao, Jiaying
Dong, Shuangshi
coredata.dc:description: In this work, oxygen defect-rich pencil-like ZnO nanorods were prepared by a simple hydrothermal method, and Ag and CDots co-catalysts were further introduced for modification. The morphology, structure, UV–vis diffuse reflectance, photoluminescence (PL) and electrochemical properties of the as-prepared photocatalysts were studied by a series of analytical techniques and measurements. The results indicated that pencil-like morphology and the existence of oxygen defects increased the carrier concentration and promoted charge carrier separation of ZnO. The introduction of Ag and CDots co-catalysts further improved the electron-hole pairs separation and visible light utilization of photocatalysts. Gaussian three-peak fitting of the PL spectra proved that the introduction of co-catalysts also affected the relative content of various oxygen defects in the catalyst. As a typical pollutant, tetracycline hydrochloride (TCH) was used to detect the photocatalytic performance of as-prepared catalysts. CDots/Ag/ZnO possessed the strongest TCH removal and mineralization performance, and the possible degradation mechanism and pathways of TCH were proposed. Furthermore, the consequence of the biotoxicity assessment showed that the ternary photocatalyst had good biocompatibility and low biotoxicity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CDots/Ag/ZnO ternary photocatalyst
Tetracycline hydrochloride
Water purification
Oxygen defect
Biotoxicity assessment

Ministry of Education of the People's Republic of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (U20A20322, 51678270) and 111 Project (B16020) of Jilin University, China.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of material synthesis process.
(a) TEM and (b-f) HR-TEM images of as-prepared CDots/Ag/ZnO sample.
(a) XRD patterns of as-prepared CDots/Ag/ZnO, (b) the Survey XPS spectra of CDots/Ag/CDots, (c-f) High-resolution XPS spectra of Zn 2p (c), O 1s (d), Ag 3d (e) and C 1s (f) in CDots/Ag/ZnO.

(a) UV–vis DRS spectra, (b) the calculated band gaps, (c) transient photocurrent response and (d) electrochemical impedance spectroscopy of as-prepared catalysts, (e) Mott-Schottky curves of ZnO and CDots/Ag/ZnO, (f) PL spectra of as-prepared catalysts.
Gaussian fits of each PL spectrum in ZnO, CDots/ZnO, Ag/ZnO and CDots/Ag/ZnO, respectively.

(a) Photocatalytic activity of different photocatalysts for TCH degradation in UV–vis light, (b) Recycling test of CDots/Ag/ZnO, (c) XRD of fresh and used CDots/Ag/ZnO photocatalyst, (d) TOC removal efficiencies of as-prepared photocatalysts, (e) Free radical trapping experiments, (f) ESR spectra of DMPO-
OH and DMPO-
O2 − adducts recorded with CDots/Ag/ZnO in the dark and under UV–vis light irradiation.
Possible degradation pathways of TCH with CDots/Ag/ZnO in the photocatalytic degradation.
Schematic photocatalytic mechanism for CDots/Ag/ZnO ternary photocatalyst.
(a-b) Relative viability of E. coli and (c-d) S. aureus after 24 h incubation with different concentrations of CDots/Ag/ZnO.
Various defects peak areas by integrating data.
Comparison of the catalyst in this work with previous ones for TCH removal.

head.ce:title: Fabrication of oxygen defect-rich pencil-like ZnO nanorods with CDots and Ag co-enhanced photocatalytic activity for tetracycline hydrochloride degradation

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Groundwater Resources and Environment (Jilin University)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, oxygen defect-rich pencil-like ZnO nanorods were prepared by a simple hydrothermal method, and Ag and CDots co-catalysts were further introduced for modification. The morphology, structure, UV–vis diffuse reflectance, photoluminescence (PL) and electrochemical properties of the as-prepared photocatalysts were studied by a series of analytical techniques and measurements. The results indicated that pencil-like morphology and the existence of oxygen defects increased the carrier concentration and promoted charge carrier separation of ZnO. The introduction of Ag and CDots co-catalysts further improved the electron-hole pairs separation and visible light utilization of photocatalysts. Gaussian three-peak fitting of the PL spectra proved that the introduction of co-catalysts also affected the relative content of various oxygen defects in the catalyst. As a typical pollutant, tetracycline hydrochloride (TCH) was used to detect the photocatalytic performance of as-prepared catalysts. CDots/Ag/ZnO possessed the strongest TCH removal and mineralization performance, and the possible degradation mechanism and pathways of TCH were proposed. Furthermore, the consequence of the biotoxicity assessment showed that the ternary photocatalyst had good biocompatibility and low biotoxicity.

ce:section.ce:para: As a kind of typical water pollutant, tetracycline hydrochloride (TCH) is widely used in human therapy and livestock industry due to its broad-spectrum antimicrobial activity [1]. However, TCH cannot be absorbed and digested by organisms, leading to its entering to the food chain and negative impact on human health [2]. Therefore, finding an environmentally friendly and efficient approach to remove TCH from water is of great significance. Compared with conventional technologies, semiconductor photocatalysis is regarded as an increasingly feasible environmental purification technology due to its advantages of high catalytic activity, chemical stability and low toxicity [3]. Thus, the development of a suitable photocatalyst is particularly indispensable.

Compared to other metal oxide semiconductors, ZnO has attracted great attention in photocatalysis field for its direct wide band gap (3.37 eV) and high exciton binding energy (60 meV) [4]. However, unitary ZnO photocatalyst possesses low visible light utilization and high exciton recombination, which restrict its photocatalytic degradation property [5]. To overcome these bottlenecks, the introduction of tailored defects and co-catalysts has been widely reported [6]. It was demonstrated that introducing oxygen defects is an effective strategy to enhance the photocatalytic performance under visible light in that they could not only narrow the band gap of ZnO, but also trap the photogenerated electrons/holes and effectively minimize the recombination of electron-hole pairs [7]. The existence of oxygen defects also provides more reactive sites for degradation, which is more conducive to the removal of pollutants [8]. At present, many methods that could successfully introduce oxygen defects have been reported, such asthermal treatment, chemical reduction, photo-reduction, etc. [9]. However, these methods are relatively complicated or costly. Also, the regulation and action mechanism of different types of oxygen defects have not been well elucidated. Therefore, there is still a great need to design a simple method that can be applied to prepare oxygen defect-rich ZnO and further explain the influence of various oxygen defects on photocatalysis.

The construction of co-catalysts is an effective method to further improve the photocatalytic performance [10]. The Surface Plasmon Resonance (SPR) effect of noble metal could increase electron density under visible light by generating hot electrons [11]. Therefore, Ag nanoparticles (NPs) supported on the surface of ZnO could significantly enhance the metal-semiconductor interfacial charge-transfer kinetics [12]. Besides, Carbon nanodots (CDots) also possess excellent electron transfer and visible light utilization property [13]. The upconversion luminescent performance enables CDots to improve the visible light utilization of pure semiconductor [14]. Therefore, we infer that the introduction of CDots and Ag co-catalysts is feasible to enhance the photocatalytic performance of ZnO, it may also tune the ratio of various oxygen defects in ZnO, thus improving the photocatalytic behavior of ternary CDots/Ag/ZnO composite.

Herein, we have synthesized a kind of oxygen defect-rich ZnO nanorods by a simple hydrothermal method for the photocatalytic degradation of TCH, and the photocatalytic performance could be further improved by CDots and Ag co-modification. A series of characterizations were conducted to analyze the phase structure, morphologies, structures, optical, electrochemical property and the cytotoxicity of the as-prepared photocatalysts [15]. Besides, the superiority of oxygen defect-rich pencil-like structure of ZnO was clarified, and the positive significance of co-modification of CDots and Ag NPs for the improvement of photocatalytic activity was proposed. Compared to the previously reported correlation catalysts, CDots/Ag/ZnO ternary material exhibited excellent TCH photocatalytic degradation and mineralization performance, and the possible degradation mechanism and pathways of TCH have been put forward [16,17,18,19].

All reagents were used directly without further purification. CDots was synthesized by hydrothermal method according to previously reported literature [20]. The photocatalyst preparation process was illustrated in Fig. 1 . The specific synthetic process of ZnO was as follows: 6 mmol of Zn(NO3)2·6H2O and hexamethylenetetramine were dissolved in 120 mL of deionized (DI) water. The solution was heated in a Teflon-lined autoclave at 100 °C for 12 h. The precipitation was collected and washed with DI water and ethanol successively before drying at 50 °C overnight to obtain ZnO nanorods. Ag NPs were in situ photo-deposited on the surface of ZnO rods. 40 mL of 0.2 mM AgNO3 solution was mixed with 0.64 mL of ethylene glycol solution (ethylene glycol in DI water at 2:1 (v:v)). Then, 0.1 g of prepared ZnO nanorods was dispersed in the above solution, followed by irradiation under 150 W xenon lamp (CEL-HXF 300, Beijing Education Au-light Co., Ltd) with continuous stirring for 60 min. The obtained sample was denoted as Ag/ZnO. 0.08 g of the Ag/ZnO powder was dispersed in 40 mL of 0.02 g/L CDots aqueous solution under continuous stirring at 60 °C for 60 min. Then the precipitate was collected and washed with DI water and ethanol separately, and finally dried at 50 °C overnight to obtain CDots/Ag/ZnO.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JEOL JSM-7800F), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), high-resolution TEM (HRTEM, via a 200 kV FEI-Tecnai F20, USA) and scanning TEM (STEM)-EDX elemental mapping (via a 200 kV FEI-Tecnai F20, USA) were examined to investigate the morphology and structure of the photocatalyst. X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements were performed by using a D8 Advance wide-angle X-ray diffractometer (Bruker, Germany) with CuKα (λ = 0.15406 nm) radiation. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was detected via a VG ESCALAB210 (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) XPS system by using an Al Kα (h = 1486.6 eV) radiation excitation source. All obtained spectra were calibrated against the C 1s signal at 284.6 eV. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a VERTEX70 spectrophotometer (Bruker, Germany) using pressed KBr discs. UV−vis diffuse reflection spectra (DRS) were taken on a Lambda 750 spectrophotometer (Perking Elmer). The photoluminescence (PL) spectra were recorded on a luminescence spectrometer (Cary Eclipse, Varian Medical Systems, USA). Electron spin resonance/Electron paramagnetic resonance (ESR/EPR) spectra were performed on a E500-10/12 ERP CW (Bruker, Germany) by using the spin-trap reagent DMPO (5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide, Sigma Chemical Co.) in water (for hydroxyl radical) and methanol (for superoxide radical). TCH degradation products were detected by LC-MS system (Agilent 1290II-6460) equipped with a Poroshell 120 EC-C18 (2.1 × 100 mm, 1.9 μm).

The transient photo-current, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and Mott-Schottky curves of the materials were measured via an electrochemical workstation (CHI-660e, Shanghai Chenhua, China). The Pt electrode, Ag/AgCl electrode and Na2SO4 (0.1 M) were used as the counter electrode, the reference electrode and the electrolyte, respectively. The prepared photocatalysts were spin-coated onto the fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) coated glass as the working electrode.

A 150 W xenon lamp (wavelength range: 300–780 nm, CEL-HXF 300, Beijing Education Au-light Co., Ltd) was chosen as the light source and tetracycline was used as the target pollutant. In a typical photocatalytic degradation test, 15 mg of photocatalyst was completely mixed with 50 mL of 30 mg/L tetracycline solution. Prior to irradiation, the suspension was thoroughly stirred for 30 min in the dark to achieve an adsorption-desorption equilibrium. During the irradiation, 2 mL of the suspension was taken every 10 min and filtrated by a 0.22 μm membrane. The stability and repeatability of the photocatalysts were tested by repeating the experiments for 5 times. The TOC removal efficiencies of as-prepared photocatalysts were determined by a TOC analyzer (TOC, SSM-5000A). All degradation related experiments in our work were conducted at least triplicate parallel experiments and corresponding error bars were shown in the performance plots.

The scavenger quenching experiments were carried out by a controlled variable method, and the capture agent was added before stirring in dark. Potassium bromate (10.0 mM, Sigma), formic acid (10.0 mM, Sigma), isopropanol (10.0 mM, Sigma) and p-benzoquinone (1.0 mM, Sigma) were applied as the scavengers for electron (e−), holes (h+), hydroxyl radicals (
OH) and superoxide radicals (
O2 −), respectively [21,22,23,24].

The biotoxicity of the CDots/Ag/ZnO ternary photocatalyst was tested by using E. coli and S. aureus as substrate [15]. The circular filter paper with diameter of 6 mm was immersed in composite suspension with different concentrations (0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8 and 1 g/L). Thereafter, the impregnated filter paper was placed on a Mueller-Hinton agar (MHA) inoculated with bacteria and cultured at 37 °C for 24 h.

SEM image of as-prepared ZnO (Fig. S1) displayed the hexagonal bottom pencil-like morphology, which could be explained by the Gibbs-Curie-Wulff theorem. Specifically, the surface energy (E) of the facets in ZnO rods followed the sequence of E[0 0 0 1] > E[0 1 ī ī] > E[0 1 ī 0] > E[0 1 ī 1] > E[0 0 0 ī], indicating the highest crystal growth velocity of (0 0 0 1) plane. Therefore, the crystal growth of (0 0 0 1) was accompanied by the formation of pencil-like tip [25]. This would produce higher carrier concentration when excited by external radiation due to its reduced diameter [26]. For the TEM image of CDots/Ag/ZnO composite showed in Fig. 2 a, it could be observed that Ag and CDots co-catalysts were uniformly attached to the surface of ZnO, rather than entering into the lattice. The clear lattice fringed at a spacing of 0.248 nm in Fig. 2b, corresponding to the (101) lattice plane of ZnO, while the HR-TEM image also showed the presence of pores in pencil-like ZnO, which could increase the surface area of the photocatalyst and promote the pollutants degradation [27]. The Ag NP presented two lattice spacings of 0.204 and 0.236 nm, corresponding to the (200) and (111) planes, respectively (Fig. 2c and d). The lattice spacing of 0.212 nm confirmed the existence of CDots (Fig. 2e and f) [10]. Additionally, STEM-EDX elemental mapping measurement was performed to determine the distributions of the Zn, O, Ag and C elements in CDots/Ag/ZnO. The results were shown in Fig. S1c–f, respectively. All the elements could be clearly observed in the images, demonstrating the successful introduction of the Ag NPs and CDots on the surface of ZnO rods. The intimate contact between ZnO rod, Ag NPs and CDots was beneficial for electron transfer, thus increasing the charge separation rate and photocatalytic activity [28].

The XRD patterns of ZnO rods, CDots/ZnO, Ag/ZnO, CDots/Ag/ZnO were shown in Fig. 3 a. The XRD pattern of ZnO rods matched well with the hexagonal phase wurtzite ZnO (ICDD: 36-1451), which were in accordance with the TEM results. After Ag deposition, there was an obvious decrease in the peak intensity for Ag/ZnO due to SPR effect. The characteristic peaks at 2θ = 38.12°, 44.30°, 64.45°, 77.41° and 81.55° were corresponding to the (111), (200), (220), (311) and (222) planes of Ag NPs (ICDD: 4-0783), suggesting that Ag NPs were well-crystallized. The average crystallite size of the Ag NPs was calculated to be 47 nm according to the Debye-Scherrer formula, which was consistent with the results of TEM [29]. There was no obvious peak broadening or peak shift for ZnO with Ag deposition. This proved that the attachment of Ag nanoparticles on the surface of ZnO rods neither affected the crystallinity nor induced strain in ZnO. However, the characteristic peaks of CDots were not observed from the patterns of CDots/ZnO and CDots/Ag/ZnO because of low content and small particle sizes of CDots [30].

XPS was further used to evaluate the chemical composition of the CDots/Ag/ZnO nanocomposites. The survey spectrum (Fig. 3b) showed the existence of Zn, O, Ag and C elements in CDots/Ag/ZnO. The high-resolution XPS spectra of each element were shown in Fig. 3c-d. In Fig. 3c, the Zn 2p3/2 and Zn 2p1/2 peaks were located at 1021.2 and 1044.9 eV, respectively, and the ~23 eV interval between the two peaks confirmed the existence of Zn2+ lattice ions [29]. The XPS signals of O 1s were composed of four peaks centered at 531.1, 531.9, 532.7 and 533.3 eV, which were related to CO, ZnO crystal lattice, oxygen defects and C–OH bonds, respectively (Fig. 3d) [31]. According to the quantitative analysis of XPS spectra, the oxygen defects was accounted for 18.93% of the total O 1s peak area. This demonstrated the widespread presence of oxygen defects in the compound photocatalyst. Two peaks at 367.8 and 373.8 eV in the Ag 3d spectra (Fig. 3e) were corresponding to Ag 3d5/2 and Ag 3d3/2, indicating a metallic nature of silver [32]. Compared with Ag NPs, the Ag 3d peaks (3d5/2 at 368.2 eV and 3d3/2 at 374.2 eV) of CDots/Ag/ZnO shifted to a lower binding energy position, which was attributed to the electron transfer between Ag NPs and ZnO rods [33]. Furthermore, both Ag 3d5/2 and 3d3/2 peaks could be deconvolved into two peaks. The lower binding energy part (367.8 and 373.8 eV) was ordinarily attributed to metallic Ag, while the higher binding energy part (368.2 and 374.3 eV) was associated with Ag2O or metallic Ag [34,35]. The existence of Ag2O was probably due to the humid aerobic environment and aggregation of Ag [29,35]. The C 1s high-resolution XPS spectrum (Fig. 3f) could be fitted with four peaks at 284.4, 285.0, 286.2 and 288.6 eV, which were attributed to sp2 (CC), sp3 (C–C and C–H), C–O and CO bonds separately [36]. The C 1s XPS spectrum indicated the existence of CDots. The successful introduction of CDots was also confirmed through FT-IR results (Fig. S3).

Fig. 4 a illustrated the UV–vis DRS of as-prepared composites. The absorption band edge of ZnO rods was located at ~450 nm. In the ultraviolet region (λ < 400 nm), enhanced absorption could be found for the composites with addition of Ag or CDots. Besides, the introduction of Ag NPs significantly improved the light absorption capacity in the visible light region (400 nm < λ < 780 nm), which was attributed to the SPR effect of Ag NPs. The SPR effect caused by Ag NPs modification increased the charge density on the surface of the ZnO rod and enhanced the light absorption capacity of the semiconductor [11,37]. After the addition of CDots, the light absorption range was further expanded. This could be attributed to the good upconversion luminescence performance of CDots for the effective utilization of long-wavelength photons, which will provide more carriers for photocatalytic reactions [38,39]. The energy band gaps (Eg) of the pristine ZnO rods and CDots/Ag/ZnO were calculated to be 3.16 eV and 3.08 eV, respectively (Fig. 4b). Compared with the bulk ZnO (3.37 eV), the decreased band gap of ZnO rods could be attributed to the formation of oxygen defects which was demonstrated in the previous study and was in accordance with the XPS results (Fig. 3d). The reduction of the band gap could improve the utilization of visible light and had a positive impact on the photocatalytic degradation performance [6,7]. The modification of Ag NPs and CDots synergistically improved the visible light utilization efficiency of composite, and macroscopically appeared as a decrease of the band gap of ternary catalyst.

The transient photocurrent response curves of as-prepared photocatalysts were shown in Fig. 4c. ZnO rods showed low photocurrent response due to the narrow light absorption range (<450 nm, in Fig. 4a). After the deposition of Ag NPs or CDots, the Ag/ZnO and CDots/ZnO exhibited the steady and reproducible photocurrent response during several consecutive on-off irradiation cycles. Compared with binary catalysts, the photocurrent response intensity of the CDots/Ag/ZnO photocatalyst was significantly enhanced. The SPR effect of Ag NPs and the light utilization efficiency improved by the upconversion performance of CDots synergistically increased the carrier concentration on the semiconductor surface. At the same time, Ag NPs and CDots both revealed excellent charge transfer capability and further reduced the rate of exciton recombination efficiency, which enabled more photogenerated electrons to be used [12,38]. The results confirmed the excellent synergy of CDots and Ag NPs. The EIS plots in Fig. 4d further revealed the transfer properties of the photogenerated charge carriers. The semicircle radius for the materials increased in the order of CDots/Ag/ZnO < Ag/ZnO < CDots/ZnO < ZnO, suggesting the ternary photocatalyst possesses the highest efficiency for separation and transfer of photogenerated charge carriers [30].

The positive slope of Mott-Schottky curves confirmed the n-type semiconductors of as-prepared ZnO and CDots/Ag/ZnO (Fig. 4e) [40]. Generally, for an n-type semiconductor, the flat band potential could be considered as the conduction band (CB) bottom potential [41]. Therefore, the CB potential of the CDots/Ag/ZnO ternary catalyst was 0.08 eV lower than that of the ZnO rods. Since the mathematical relationship between the ZnO semiconductor valence band (VB) energy value (EVB), band gap (Eg) and CB energy value (ECB) could be simply described as: EVB = Eg + ECB. Combined with the results in Fig. 4b, it could be concluded that the introduction of Ag and CDots did not change the VB potential of the semiconductor. Fig. 4f presented the PL spectra of photocatalysts with the excitation wavelength at 325 nm. The curve for ZnO rods consisted of two main parts, one of which was centered in the UV region (385 nm) and the other was an intense defect-related band (440–750 nm) in the visible light region [4]. The former peak was assigned to the near band edge emission due to the recombination of free excitons in ZnO nanorod. The strong and wide emission in the range 440–750 nm was attributed to the convolution of defect peaks such as oxygen vacancy (Vo) and interstitial oxygen (Oi), etc. [42]. The PL spectra of binary heterostructures in CDots/ZnO and Ag/ZnO confirmed that the deposition of Ag or CDots could suppress the PL intensity, suggesting the reduced electron-hole pairs recombination. For CDots/ZnO, the conjugated π structure of CDots provided the photogenerated electrons transfer and separation centers, which could enhance the separation efficiency of the photo-excited electron-hole pairs [43]. The markedly reduced electron-hole pairs recombination rate of Ag/ZnO was attributed to the SPR effect and superior electron transfer capability of Ag NPs [5]. The lowest PL intensity for CDots/Ag/ZnO composites indicated the highest the separation rate of photo-induced charge carriers, which could result from the synergistic effect of two dopants.

To further explore the defect types of various photocatalysts, a three-peak Gaussian fitting method was adopted in the visible emission region for PL spectra. It could be seen in Fig. 5 that all photocatalysts presented three deconvolution peaks in blue-green (~520 nm), yellow-green (~570 nm), and orange-red (~630 nm) regions, which originated from singly charged oxygen vacancy (Vo +), doubly charged oxygen vacancy (Vo ++), and interstitial oxygen (Oi), respectively [4,42]. The relative proportions of the respective peak areas in the as-prepared photocatalysts were also shown in Table 1 . In comparison to ZnO, the relative content of Oi decreased significantly after the introduction of Ag and/or CDots, while the relative content of Vo + increased remarkably, and the Vo ++ relative content remained unchanged. EPR characterization results also verified the increase in Vo + content (Fig. S4). The results were in accordance with the previous study, as noble metal deposition could increase relative content of the Vo [44]. The introduction of CDots could tune the relative contents of various oxygen defects, either. Therefore, the ternary CDots/Ag/ZnO showed the highest Vo + content and the lowest Oi content in comparison with single and binary photocatalysts. It has been reported that the charge separation efficiency of Vo is higher than that of Oi due to the existence of a potential barrier in ZnO rods, leading to the enhanced photocatalytic activity for the ZnO rod based photocatalyst with higher Vo content [45]. Besides, the relationship between Vo + and Vo ++ was as follows [46]:
Vo + + O2 → Vo ++ +
O2 −
Vo ++ + e− CB → Vo +

It could be seen that the presence of Vo + promotes the generation of superoxide radicals, while the produced Vo ++ was beneficial to capture electrons and improved the charge separation. Therefore, these effects synergistically contributed improvements on the photocatalytic performance of the ternary CDots/Ag/ZnO.

The photocatalytic activity of the as-prepared ZnO and the composite was explored for TCH degradation under UV–vis light. As observed in Fig. 6 a, the order of TCH removal efficiencies was CDots/Ag/ZnO > Ag/ZnO > CDots/ZnO > ZnO. For the as-prepared ZnO, only 81.4% of tetracycline could be degraded in 60 min. The introduction of Ag or CDots could improve the degradation efficiency, where 90.4% and 85.0% of TCH were decomposed, respectively. The ternary photocatalyst showed the highest photocatalytic activity with the degradation efficiency of 96.0% in 60 min, which was due to the strongest light absorption and the excellent electron transfer capability. The TCH degradation performance of CDots/Ag/ZnO was also compared with other previously reported photocatalysts in Table 2 . Under the condition of less catalyst dosage, the as-prepared ternary photocatalyst exhibited comparative TCH degradation performance than other catalysts. To quantify the photocatalytic activity, the degradation kinetics (Fig. S5) were calculated via the Langmuir-Hinshelwood model. The CDots/Ag/ZnO showed the highest reaction rate constant (k, 0.0489 min−1) than those of Ag/ZnO (0.0339 min−1), CDots/ZnO (0.0267 min−1) and pristine ZnO (0.0228 min−1), indicating the synergistic effect of Ag and CDots. The ternary photocatalyst also showed great stability and reusability. The degradation rate remained at 91.47% after five repeated experiments (Fig. 6b). In addition, the XRD spectrum of the composite did not present noticeable change after photocatalytic reaction (Fig. 6c), indicating its stable phase structure. To further explore the mineralization performance of the catalysts, the TOC removal efficiencies for TCH were obtained (Fig. 6d). Pristine ZnO had 27.21% TOC removal efficiency for TCH. After the modification of co-catalysts, ternary material demonstrated the highest TCH mineralization rate of 68.82%, which was also consistent with the degradation efficiencies.

To reveal the photocatalytic mechanism of CDots/Ag/ZnO, free radical trapping experiments were conducted to identify the reactive species during the TCH degradation, as shown in Fig. 6e. The effect of the quenchers on removal efficiency of TCH ranked in the order of formic acid (for h+) > p-benzoquinone (for
O2 −) > isopropanol (for
OH) > potassium bromate (for e−). The effect of e− on the TCH photocatalytic degradation was negligible. To further investigate the free radicals involved in degradation, the ESR spin-trap technique was performed with DMPO as a radical scavenger of
OH and
O2 −. As shown in Fig. 6f, no signal was detected for the ternary CDots/Ag/ZnO in the dark. After UV–vis light irradiation, four characteristic peaks with intensity ratio at 1: 2: 2: 1 were observed for the DMPO-
OH adducts in water. Besides, six characteristic peaks of DMPO-
O2 − adducts in methanol were spotted. All the results were in consistent with the previous literature [47]. These results indicated the formation of
OH and
O2 − radicals under UV–vis light irradiating. Combined with the results of free radical trapping experiments, it could be concluded that the h+,
OH and
O2 − play important roles in the process of TCH degradation.

During the photocatalytic degradation process, TCH molecule structure would be attacked by reactive oxidation species and gradually broken into smaller molecular structures. In order to further shed light on the degradation pathway and intermediates information of TCH, we performed LC-MS characterization. The possible molecular structures of the intermediate products were listed in Table S1 and degradation pathways of TCH were displayed in Fig. 7 . First, the TCH molecule (m/z = 445) experienced the process of hydroxylation/dehydroxylation, N-demethylation and deamination and was transformed into P1 (m/z = 413), P2 (m/z = 429), P3 (m/z = 475) and P4 (m/z = 417) [48,49,50,51]. Afterwards, the intermediate products of larger molecular weight undergoed the above-mentioned similar reactions and ring-opening reactions [52,53,54,55]. Under the joint reaction of h+/
O2 −/
OH, they were further destroyed to form small molecular organic compounds, such as P14 (m/z = 59), P15 (m/z = 102), P16 (m/z = 99) and P17 (m/z = 78), which were finally converted into NH4 +, CO2 and H2O.

Based on the above results, the photocatalytic mechanism of CDots/Ag/ZnO ternary photocatalyst was proposed in Fig. 8 . Under UV–vis light irradiation, the electrons in ZnO rods were excited from valence band to conduction band. And the Vo and Oi defects in CDots/Ag/ZnO could serve as electron acceptors and hole trappers, respectively. These defects further modulated the band gap and inhibited the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, thereby minimizing the energy consumption caused by exciton recombination. Among the as-prepared photocatalysts, the relative content of Vo + was the highest in CDots/Ag/ZnO, which was more conducive to the generation of
O2 − [56]. Meanwhile, the SPR effect of Ag and the upconversion luminescent performance of CDots synergistically enhanced the visible light utilization of CDots/Ag/ZnO ternary photocatalyst. The hot electrons generated via the SPR effect of Ag NPs and electrons migrated from ZnO rods were captured by oxidized substances in the solution. And the accumulated photogenerated electrons could be stored and transferred by CDots. Therefore, the efficient separation of the photogenerated electron-hole pairs were improved at the interface of the CDots/Ag/ZnO photocatalyst. According to the analysis of DRS and Mott-Schottky results (Fig. 4b and e), the band gap and flat band potential of the CDots/Ag/ZnO were 3.08 eV and −0.68 eV, respectively. Photogenerated electrons in the CB of CDots/Ag/ZnO could be captured by O2 to generate
O2 – radical, as the flat band potential (−0.68 eV, vs. NHE) is more negative than the redox potential of O2/
O2 − (−0.046 eV, vs. NHE) [57]. Besides, the VB bottom potential (2.40 eV vs. NHE) was positive than the redox potentials of OH–/
OH (2.38 eV vs. NHE), leading to photocatalytic degradation the TCH effectively. The reaction could be described as following:
O2 + eCB − →
O2 −
hVB + + OH– →
OH

The toxicity assessment of nanomaterials was particularly important for their potential application in environmental purification. As two kinds of typical Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria, the E. coli (Fig. 9 a and b) and S. aureus (Fig. 9c and d) were used to investigate the possible cytotoxicity of as-prepared CDots/Ag/ZnO. Compared to tetracycline, there was almost no observable inhibition for E. coli and S. aureus, even when the dosage of the photocatalyst was as high as 10 g L−1. The results demonstrated the low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility of CDots/Ag/ZnO composite.

In summary, oxygen defect-rich pencil-like ZnO nanorods were successfully prepared by a simple hydrothermal method and further co-modified by CDots and Ag NPs. Due to the reduced diameter of pencil-like tip morphology and tuned oxygen defect species, the ternary photocatalyst with high relative content of Vo + showed the highest tetracycline removal rate of 94.95% under UV–vis light. Besides, the recycling test revealed the high stability of the CDots/Ag/ZnO. Through free radical capture experiments and analysis of ESR, it was further concluded that h+,
O2 − and
OH were the active radicals in photocatalytic degradation. The prepared composite photocatalyst also showed low biological toxicity, which was beneficial for its further application.

Tianren Li: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - original draft. Yinglu Liu: Investigation. Mingyu Li: Supervision. Jingjing Jiang: Visualization. Jiaying Gao: Software. Shuangshi Dong: Resources, Writing - review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (U20A20322, 51678270) and 111 Project (B16020) of Jilin University, China.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.seppur.2021.118605.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

Colloid Interface Sci., 554(2019), pp. 531-543.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.N. Liu, R.X. Wang, Z.K. Yang, H. Du, Y.F. Jiang, C.C. Shen, K. Liang, A.W. Xu, Enhanced visible-light photocatalytic activity of Z-scheme graphitic carbon nitride/oxygen vacancy-rich zinc oxide hybrid photocatalysts, Chinese J. Catal., 36(2015), pp. 2135-2144.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Degradation of refractory pollutants under solar light irradiation by a robust and self-protected ZnO/CdS/TiO2 hybrid photocatalyst

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.Y. Zhang, , W.K. Wang, D.N. Pei, H.Q. Yu, Degradation of refractory pollutants under solar light irradiation by a robust and self-protected ZnO/CdS/TiO2 hybrid photocatalyst. Water Res., 92(2016), pp. 78-86.
